target.title: fix(feishu): normalize unicode emojis to Feishu emoji type strings for reactions API (#66406)
target.body:
## Summary
Feishu reactions API returns error `231001` ("reaction type is invalid") because the agent sends raw unicode emojis (e.g. thumbs-up `👍`) but the API requires uppercase Feishu type strings like `THUMBSUP`.

## Root Cause
In `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`, the `emoji` parameter from the tool call is passed directly to `addReactionFeishu()` without conversion. The Feishu API only accepts its own emoji type vocabulary (e.g. `THUMBSUP`, `HEART`, `FIRE`) per https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce, not unicode characters.

ProjectClownfish landed a narrow repair on this branch on 2026-04-28 (force-push by `vincentkoc`) so the original contributor path can stay canonical. The fix shape and tests have been stable since then.

## Changes
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`: Add `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` function with a unicode-to-Feishu-type mapping table covering all common emoji reactions. Known Feishu type strings pass through unchanged; case-insensitive matching is supported.
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Apply `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` to the emoji parameter before calling `addReactionFeishu()` and `listReactionsFeishu()`.
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts`: Add test coverage for pass-through, case normalization, unicode conversion, whitespace trimming, and unknown emoji fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool calls that pass unicode emoji to `addReactionFeishu` send `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "👍" } }` to the Feishu reactions HTTP API, which rejects it with `code: 231001` and `msg: "reaction type is invalid"`. After the fix the same tool call sends `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "THUMBSUP" } }`, which is the documented Feishu emoji type vocabulary. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-66406` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. Because the worktree's `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` imports run-time Feishu SDK modules transitively, the smoke script extracts the **production `FeishuEmoji` constant table and `normalizeFeishuEmoji` function body verbatim** from `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` (SHA-256-pinned for integrity) and executes that exact slice via `node --experimental-strip-types`. No mocks, no test runner — the live function body is what runs. PR head: `a3bebf502f1ce0ebfe3b754ec1ab67b3302098fc`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/feishu-reaction-emoji-type && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts`. The smoke script reads `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`, slices from `export const FeishuEmoji` to end-of-file, prints the prod-slice SHA-256, then exercises `normalizeFeishuEmoji` against the actual reporter unicode emojis.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3bebf502f` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, 2245-byte production slice verified by SHA-256 `8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts
$ wc -c C:\...\feishu-combined-1778492490068.mts
  bytes: 3667
(prod slice: 2245 bytes copied verbatim from extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts starting at offset 3989)
(SHA-256 of prod slice for integrity:)
  8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54

=== Sample known Feishu emoji constants ===
FeishuEmoji.THUMBSUP = THUMBSUP
FeishuEmoji.HEART    = HEART
FeishuEmoji.CRY      = CRY

=== Unicode emoji -> Feishu type name ===
  "👍"             (thumbs up)  ->  THUMBSUP
  "❤️"             (red heart)  ->  HEART
  "😂"             (face with tears of joy)  ->  LAUGHING
  "🎉"             (party popper)  ->  PARTY
  "🙏"             (folded hands)  ->  PRAY
  "🔥"             (fire)  ->  FIRE

=== Bypass cases (must NOT be touched) ===
  "THUMBSUP"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "thumbsup"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "ThumbsUp"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "  HEART  "     -> "HEART"
  "UNKNOWN_TYPE"  -> "UNKNOWN_TYPE"

=== Request body produced for issue #66406 reproducer ===
user sends: "👍"
request body: {"reaction":{"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"}}
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: Each tested unicode emoji is mapped to a documented Feishu type name (`👍`→`THUMBSUP`, `❤️`→`HEART`, `😂`→`LAUGHING`, `🎉`→`PARTY`, `🙏`→`PRAY`, `🔥`→`FIRE`) — exactly the vocabulary listed at https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce. Existing uppercase Feishu types (`THUMBSUP`), lowercase aliases (`thumbsup`), mixed case (`ThumbsUp`), and whitespace-padded inputs (`"  HEART  "`) all pass through to their canonical type name. Unknown inputs (`UNKNOWN_TYPE`) fall through unchanged, preserving forward-compat with new Feishu types. The final reproducer line confirms the runtime request body now contains `"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"` — which is exactly what the Feishu API requires to avoid `code: 231001`.
- **What was not tested**: A live `addReactionFeishu` HTTP roundtrip to the actual Feishu Open Platform server with a paired bot token + chat_id is not exercised on the contributor machine (would need a configured Feishu workspace). The fix is a pure-function normalization at the request-builder boundary; downstream `addReactionFeishu` already serialises the `emoji_type` value verbatim, so the only behavioural change is that the request body now carries documented Feishu types instead of raw unicode. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live Feishu API recording is required; vincentkoc / ProjectClownfish already validated this fix shape via `pnpm -s vitest run extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` and the gated `pnpm check:changed` lanes when force-pushing on 2026-04-28.

## Test

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` — 5 cases (pass-through / case normalization / unicode conversion / whitespace trimming / unknown emoji fallback)
- `pnpm check:changed` — green (per vincentkoc's 2026-04-28 status update)

Closes #66406

target.title: fix(systemd): reconcile managed-env unit migrations
target.body:
## Summary

- carry `#66295` onto current `main`
- fix the two real migration regressions from review: rebuild malformed units when `ExecStart=` is missing, and reconcile stale `WorkingDirectory=` in-place instead of leaving `CHDIR` failures behind
- remove the redundant managed-env split and clean up the temp-home test leakage

Fixes #66248.
Related #66219.
Supersedes #66295.

## Why this carry exists

`#66295` had the right core direction: move OpenClaw-managed systemd env into a drop-in so upgrades stop clobbering user `EnvironmentFile=` and `Environment=` directives.

The branch still needed maintainer follow-up in three spots:

1. malformed existing units with no `ExecStart=` were no longer repaired
2. stale `WorkingDirectory=` values could survive migration and fail startup at `CHDIR`
3. the new integration tests were leaving temp dirs behind

This carry fixes those and keeps the user-preserving drop-in design intact.

## Verification

- `pnpm test:serial src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

## Changelog

Added under `## Unreleased` with:
- `Thanks @solomonneas.`
- `Carry follow-up: @vincentkoc.`

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): stop forwarding embedding dimensions upstream
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `memory-lancedb` forwarded `dimensions` to every direct OpenAI-compatible embeddings request, which breaks vLLM/NVIDIA-style providers that reject that field. - Why it matters: memory recall/search can fail at embed time for otherwise compatible providers. - What changed: the direct embeddings client now preserves server-side `dimensions` first, then retries without `dimensions` only when the provider clearly rejects that field; the fallback vector is truncated locally to the configured dimension. - Scope boundary: config shape, provider selection, secrets handling, runtime permissions, and migrations are unchanged. ## Change Type - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes none - Related none - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause - Root cause: the direct embedding wrapper assumed every OpenAI-compatible provider would accept `dimensions`, then passed it straight through upstream. - Missing guardrail: no regression test covered a provider that rejects `dimensions`, or the compatibility requirement that accepting providers still receive server-side `dimensions`. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level: Unit test - Target test file: `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts` - Scenarios locked in: - accepting OpenAI-compatible providers receive `dimensions` upstream; - providers that clearly reject the `dimensions` field are retried without it and use local truncation; - unsupported dimension values stay unavailable without a fallback retry; - short fallback vectors return the memory-recall unavailable result instead of rejecting the tool call. - Supplemental local run: ```text $ .\node_modules\.bin\vitest.CMD run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts -t "sends OpenAI embedding dimensions upstream when accepted|retries without OpenAI embedding dimensions and truncates locally when rejected|returns unavailable when fallback embedding is shorter than the configured dimensions|does not retry without dimensions when the provider rejects the dimension value" Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 4 passed | 124 skipped (128) ``` ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Memory recall still uses server-side dimensions for providers that accept them. Providers that reject `dimensions` now work through a retry without that field, as long as they return a vector long enough to truncate locally. ## Diagram ```text Before: memory_recall -> OpenAI-compatible request with dimensions -> provider rejects body.dimensions -> recall unavailable After, accepting provider: memory_recall -> request with dimensions -> provider-sized vector -> store/search After, rejecting provider: memory_recall -> request with dimensions -> provider rejects body.dimensions -> retry without dimensions -> receive full embedding -> local truncation -> store/search ``` ## Security Impact - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows - Runtime/container: local workspace with pnpm - Model/provider: `nvidia/nv-embed-v1` - Integration/channel: `memory-lancedb` plugin through real OpenClaw `ltm search` - Relevant config (redacted): `embedding.baseUrl=https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`, `embedding.model=nvidia/nv-embed-v1`, `embedding.dimensions=4` ### Steps 1. Configured `memory-lancedb` with NVIDIA's OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint and `embedding.dimensions=4`. 2. Initialized an isolated LanceDB database through the real OpenClaw plugin CLI. 3. Ran `openclaw ltm search "openclaw memory proof" --limit 1` against current main. 4. Ran the same command with the fixed branch plugin loaded. 5. Seeded the branch LanceDB database with a 4-dimensional proof vector and reran the branch search. ### Expected 1. Current main fails because it forwards `dimensions` with no fallback. 2. The fixed branch preserves server-side `dimensions` for providers that accept them. 3. For NVIDIA, the fixed branch retries without `dimensions` after the provider rejection and truncates the returned 4096-dimensional embedding locally to 4 dimensions. 4. LanceDB search succeeds with the configured 4-dimensional table. ### Actual - Current main failed with NVIDIA's live provider error: `400 ... Extra inputs are not permitted ... ('body', 'dimensions')`. - The fixed branch succeeded and returned the seeded LanceDB proof row: `proof branch locally truncated NVIDIA embedding dimensions`. - This confirms the compatibility fallback omits `dimensions` after the provider rejects them and uses local truncation before LanceDB search. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Perf numbers Current in main: After in this branch, empty DB: After in this branch, seeded DB: Earlier targeted regression test screenshot: ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: `memory-lancedb` OpenAI-compatible embeddings preserve server-side `dimensions` for providers that accept that field, while providers that reject `dimensions` can still recall/search memories through a retry without `dimensions` and local truncation of the fallback embedding. - Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw source checkout, pnpm workspace, real `memory-lancedb` plugin with LanceDB storage, NVIDIA OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint using a redacted API key, `embedding.baseUrl=https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`, `embedding.model=nvidia/nv-embed-v1`, and `embedding.dimensions=4`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configured an isolated OpenClaw runtime with the PR branch `memory-lancedb` plugin override, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`. 2. Ran `node --import tsx src/entry.ts --no-color ltm search "openclaw memory proof" --limit 1` against the fixed branch and the real NVIDIA OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint. 3. Seeded the branch LanceDB database with a 4-dimensional proof memory row and reran the same `ltm search` command. 4. Compared with current `main`, which ran the same OpenClaw/LanceDB/NVIDIA setup and failed when `dimensions` was forwarded upstream without a fallback. - Evidence after fix: The terminal screenshots embedded above in the PR Evidence section show real OpenClaw output from the branch. The empty branch run returned `[]`, and the seeded branch run returned the LanceDB proof row with `id: proof-branch-local-truncation`, `text: proof branch locally truncated NVIDIA embedding dimensions`, and `score: 1`. The current-main screenshot shows the live provider rejection: `Extra inputs are not permitted` for `body.dimensions`. - Observed result after fix: The branch OpenClaw run completed memory search successfully against LanceDB after the NVIDIA-compatible provider rejected `dimensions`, the fallback request omitted `dimensions`, and the longer embedding was truncated locally to the configured 4-dimensional table. - What was not tested: No provider selection, secrets handling, runtime permissions, migration, or config-shape changes were tested because this PR does not change those surfaces. ## Human Verification - Verified scenarios: live OpenClaw memory-lancedb search against NVIDIA's OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint with `embedding.dimensions=4`; current main fails when `dimensions` is forwarded upstream without a fallback; the fixed branch succeeds by retrying without `dimensions` and truncating locally before LanceDB search. - Edge cases checked: provider rejecting upstream `dimensions`; configured 4-dimensional LanceDB table receiving a locally truncated vector from a longer provider embedding. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: a provider may reject `dimensions` and then return an embedding shorter than the configured local dimension. - Mitigation: the code returns the existing unavailable memory result with a clear error instead of storing or searching with a malformed vector.

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```text
Enriched built-in /usage full:
  Usage: 12k in / 450 out · cache 2.0k cached / 300 new · ctx 45k/200k (23%) · model gpt-5.4 · session `agent:main:webchat:dm:123`
```

```text
Static footer composed with built-in usage:
  ok

  Usage: 12 in / 3 out
  — footer
```

```text
Usage-aware footer suppressing duplicate built-in usage:
  ok

  ↑12 ↓3
```

```text
Successful block streaming with final body suppressed:
  streamed chunk: Hello
  trailing payload returned: Usage: 12 in / 3 out · cache 4 cached / 2 new
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing
  - unresolved footer placeholders remain literal, while unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders are blanked
  - successful block streaming can return only a trailing footer payload without prepending a verbose new-session notice
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
  - live Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp channel behavior
  - message-tool outbound send behavior (`#39913` remains intentionally out of scope)
  - screenshot-based verification in a running OpenClaw instance

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:
  - Enabling both built-in `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` could produce confusing duplicate footer lines.
  - Mitigation: Added explicit composition rules and tests that suppress the separate built-in usage line when the footer already consumes usage-aware placeholders.
- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:
  - Block-streaming channels may render the usage/footer as a separate trailing message rather than mutating the already-streamed body.
  - Mitigation: The implementation preserves delivery without trying to retroactively rewrite the streamed content, and dedicated reply-runner coverage locks in the suppressed-final-payload success path.
- Risk:
  - Inherited default semantics could confuse users if explicit `off` is not distinguishable from “unset.”
  - Mitigation: Explicit `off` is preserved in session state and TUI patching, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` remains the clear-to-inherit path; both behaviors are covered by tests and docs.

target.title: feat(gateway): add MCP Apps protocol extension (tools, resources, UI meta)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Gateway clients that want to build MCP-powered app experiences (tool palettes, embedded HTML panels, resource browsers) have no typed WebSocket RPC surface for discovering, invoking, and reading MCP tools and resources.
- **Why it matters:** Without a first-class gateway protocol, every client must reverse-engineer the HTTP loopback interface or poll for tool state, losing type safety and real-time event delivery.
- **What changed:** Added four new WS methods (`mcp.tools.list`, `mcp.tools.call`, `mcp.resources.list`, `mcp.resources.read`), a `mcp.tool.result` gateway event, typed TypeBox schemas with AJV validators, a 30-second tool-resolution cache, an in-process builtin HTML resource registry, `_meta.ui` on MCP tool schemas, HTTP loopback `resources/list` and `resources/read` handlers, scope assignments, and two docs (client guide + implementation reference).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the existing MCP HTTP loopback tool-call path, no changes to agent execution or tool approval flows, no new npm dependencies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: MCP Apps gateway protocol extension
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — new feature.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — new feature, no regression. New test coverage added:

- **37 new tests** across 3 test files
- `mcp-app-resources.test.ts` (14 tests): builtin resource registry CRUD, size rejection, URI routing
- `mcp.test.ts` (11 tests): WS handler dispatch for all 4 methods, scope enforcement, session key resolution, error paths
- `mcp-http.test.ts` (12 tests, 5 new): HTTP loopback `resources/list`, `resources/read`, capability advertisement, missing/unknown URI handling

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway WebSocket clients can now call `mcp.tools.list`, `mcp.tools.call`, `mcp.resources.list`, and `mcp.resources.read` methods.
- Gateway emits `mcp.tool.result` events after tool calls complete.
- MCP HTTP loopback `initialize` response now advertises `resources` capability.
- MCP HTTP loopback supports `resources/list` and `resources/read` requests.
- MCP tool schemas now include optional `_meta.ui` metadata (width, height, title) for tools that declare `mcpAppUi`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[WS client] -> [no mcp.* methods available]
[HTTP client] -> [initialize: {tools}] -> [tools/list, tools/call only]

After:
[WS client] -> mcp.tools.list -> {tools, toolSchema}
[WS client] -> mcp.tools.call -> mcp.tool.result event
[WS client] -> mcp.resources.list -> {resources}
[WS client] -> mcp.resources.read -> {contents}
[HTTP client] -> [initialize: {tools, resources}] -> [resources/list, resources/read added]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No** — uses existing READ_SCOPE / WRITE_SCOPE
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** — all in-process, no external calls
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** — `mcp.tools.call` delegates to existing tool execution pipeline
- Data access scope changed? **No** — resources are builtin, registered in-process only
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime: Node 22+
- Model/provider: N/A (protocol-level, model-independent)

### Steps

1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
2. `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/mcp.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-app-resources.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
3. Verify all 37 tests pass

### Expected

- All tests pass, build succeeds, no type errors in touched files

### Actual

- 37/37 tests pass, `pnpm build` succeeds, `pnpm tsgo` shows zero errors for our files

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```
Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  37 passed (37)
  Start at  21:26:30
  Duration  833ms
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: all 4 WS methods (list tools, call tool, list resources, read resource), HTTP loopback resources, builtin resource registration/rejection, tool cache TTL, scope assignments, error paths
- Edge cases checked: oversized HTML rejection at registration time, missing URI, unknown URI, invalid session keys, empty tool lists
- What I did **not** verify: end-to-end with a live gateway WebSocket client (protocol-level tests only)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — purely additive new methods and event
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `McpLoopbackToolCache` 30s TTL could serve stale tool lists if tools change frequently during development
  - Mitigation: TTL is short (30s); cache is per-WS-session; clients can re-call `mcp.tools.list` to force refresh
- Risk: Builtin resource HTML size limit (512 KB) could be too small for complex app UIs
  - Mitigation: Limit is enforced at registration time with a clear error; can be adjusted if needed

target.title: feat(heartbeat): support model fallbacks via {primary,fallbacks} (#69434)
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept `agents.*.heartbeat.model` as `{ primary, fallbacks }` in addition to the existing string form, mirroring `agents.defaults.model`. Closes #69434.
- Heartbeat ticks now fail over to the next fallback on retriable provider errors (rate-limit/429, overload/502/503, timeout) so primary-provider quota exhaustion no longer silently stalls the heartbeat loop for hours.
- String form stays fully backwards-compatible; new object form is additive.

## Design

- Schema: `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` reuses `AgentModelSchema` (the same union already used by `agents.defaults.model`, `imageModel`, etc.), and `src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts` replaces `model?: string` with `model?: AgentModelConfig`.
- Plumbing: `heartbeat-runner.ts` resolves primary + fallbacks via the shared `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue` / `resolveAgentModelFallbackValues` helpers and passes them through a new `GetReplyOptions.heartbeatModelFallbacks` to the reply pipeline. `get-reply-run.ts` then stores them on a new `FollowupRun.run.modelFallbacksOverride` field. `resolveModelFallbackOptions` (used by both `agent-runner-execution.ts` and the memory flush path) and the inline fallback in `followup-runner.ts` prefer this override over `resolveRunModelFallbacksOverride`, so the existing `runWithModelFallback` machinery and its retriable-error classifier (429/502/503/timeout) transparently pick up the heartbeat chain.
- `get-reply.ts` switches the defaults-fallback read to `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue(agentCfg?.heartbeat?.model)` so object-form defaults resolve correctly.
- `HeartbeatSummary` (consumed by `/status`, `/health`, compaction diagnostics, run attempts) keeps `model?: string` (now the primary) and gains an optional `modelFallbacks?: string[]` — no existing reader breaks.
- `gateway/model-pricing-cache.ts` migrates `heartbeat.model` registration from `addResolvedModelRef` to `addModelListLike`, so heartbeat fallbacks get the same pricing prefetch treatment as the agent-level model list.
- Empty `fallbacks: []` in the object form disables the fallback chain for that heartbeat without replacing it.

## Docs / config help

- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` documents both forms and the retriable-error failover semantics.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` + `src/config/schema.labels.ts` add help text and labels for `heartbeat.model`, `.primary`, and `.fallbacks` at both defaults and per-agent scopes.
- Changelog entry under `## Unreleased` → `### Changes`.

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests in `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.model-override.test.ts`:
  - passes primary + fallbacks when `heartbeat.model` is an object
  - omits fallbacks when object form has no `fallbacks` array
  - propagates per-agent heartbeat fallbacks after merging defaults
- [x] 11 pre-existing heartbeat-model-override tests still pass (string form regression guard)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:prod` (core + extensions) green
- [x] `pnpm lint:core` 0/0
- [x] `pnpm format:check` clean
- [x] `pnpm test:changed` — 2248 tests passing across 246 files
- [x] `pnpm config:schema:gen` + `pnpm config:docs:gen` baselines regenerated and checked in
- [ ] Manual: verify a 429-exhausted primary provider actually fails over to a second provider in a live tick (covered indirectly through `runWithModelFallback` unit tests + `heartbeat-runner` tests; end-to-end live repro is in the reporter's production environment)

## Notes

- Pre-commit hook was bypassed via `FAST_COMMIT=1` (documented escape hatch) to avoid a pre-existing `src/entry.version-fast-path.test.ts:44` test-type failure on an untouched file. All other gates (format, lint, core typecheck, tests, doc baselines) pass.

target.title: fix: resolve SecretRef keychain tokens in desktop app browser proxy
target.body:
## Problem

When `gateway.auth.token` is configured as a SecretRef (e.g., `{source: "exec", provider: "keychain", id: "gateway-auth-token"}`), the macOS desktop app fails to resolve the actual token value. This causes:

1. Desktop app reads `gateway.auth.token` as a dictionary, not a string
2. The `as? String` cast fails → token resolves to nil
3. Browser proxy requests to the gateway control server have no auth header
4. Gateway returns 401 Unauthorized
5. All browser tool calls via `node.invoke` fail with `UNAVAILABLE: Unauthorized`

## Fix

- Detect when `gateway.auth.token` or `gateway.auth.password` is a SecretRef dictionary with `provider: "keychain"`
- Resolve the actual value from the macOS keychain using `/usr/bin/security find-generic-password`
- Fall through to nil if resolution fails (preserving existing behavior)

## Testing

- Verified the bug locally: `security find-generic-password -s gateway-auth-token -w` returns empty (wrong lookup), while `-s openclaw -a gateway-auth-token -w` returns the actual token
- Confirmed browser control server accepts the token when passed via `Authorization: Bearer <token>`
- The fix resolves the token correctly using the `service + account` keychain lookup pattern

target.title: feat: split model routing for tool-call turns
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `model.tool` config alongside `model.primary` so tool-call turns route to a different model than chat turns
- Enables cost-efficient setups like local Ollama for chat + Claude API for tool execution
- Wraps the session `StreamFn` to detect tool-continuation turns and swap models transparently

## Motivation

Small local models (7b-14b) are fast and free but can't reliably use tools. API models handle tools perfectly but cost money. This feature lets users get the best of both worlds within a single conversation:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "ollama/qwen2.5-7b-fast",
        "tool": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Chat turns use the primary model. When the assistant triggers tool use, the tool model handles tool-call turns. Multi-step tool chains stay on the tool model until the chain completes.

## Changes

**Config layer:**
- `src/config/types.agents-shared.ts` — Added `tool?: string` to `AgentModelConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.agent-model.ts` — Added `tool` to zod schema (`.strict()` preserved)
- `src/config/model-input.ts` — Added `resolveAgentModelToolValue()` resolver

**Resolution layer:**
- `src/agents/agent-scope.ts` — Added `resolveConfiguredToolModel()` with per-agent override + global defaults

**Routing layer:**
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/split-model-stream.ts` — New `StreamFn` wrapper that detects tool-continuation turns (last message role is `"tool"` or `"toolResult"`) and routes to the tool model
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` — Resolves tool model alongside primary, passes through to attempt
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/types.ts` — Added optional `toolModel`, `toolModelProvider`, `toolModelId` to attempt params
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` — Wires split model wrapper into the `streamFn` chain

## How it works

```
User prompt → Primary Model (local/cheap)
                    │
                    ├─ No tool calls → respond directly
                    │
                    └─ Tool calls detected → Tool Model (API/capable)
                                                  │
                                                  ├─ More tool calls → stay on tool model
                                                  └─ Done → next user turn uses primary
```

The routing is implemented as a `StreamFn` wrapper positioned after message sanitization but before timeout detection in the wrapper chain. This means tool-continuation turns get all the same message cleanup (thinking block removal, tool call ID sanitization, etc.) before the routing decision.

## Test plan

- [x] 8 tests for `resolveAgentModelToolValue()` config resolver
- [x] 6 tests for `AgentModelSchema` zod validation with `tool` field
- [x] 6 tests for `wrapStreamFnWithSplitModelRouting()` stream wrapper (chat routing, tool routing, model swap-back, empty messages, options passthrough)
- [ ] Manual integration test with Ollama primary + Anthropic tool model
- [ ] Full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` (in progress)

## Known limitations

1. **Context window mismatch**: Session uses primary model's context budget for compaction regardless of which model is active
2. **Auth profile routing**: Tool model uses primary model's auth resolution; separate auth profiles not yet supported
3. **Fallback interaction**: Tool model failures fall back to primary's fallback chain, not a tool-specific chain

## AI-assisted PR 🤖

- [x] **AI-assisted**: Built with Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6)
- [x] **Degree of testing**: Unit tests fully passing (20/20). Manual integration test pending.
- [x] **I understand what the code does**: The `StreamFn` wrapper intercepts every LLM call within the session's tool-execution loop. It checks if the last message in the conversation context has role `"tool"` or `"toolResult"` — if so, the tool model `Model` object is substituted for the primary. The wrapper is stateless per-call (inspects messages each time) and positioned in the streamFn chain after message sanitization but before timeout/logging wrappers.
- [x] Session logs available on request

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(logging): support root log file overrides
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had a root `--log-level` override, but no matching root `--log-file` override, and the logger/status helpers did not honor `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE`
- Why it matters: multi-instance or service deployments need per-process log isolation without rewriting shared config files just to move one process's file sink
- What changed: added root `--log-file <path>`, propagated it into `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE` during CLI preaction, taught logger/path helpers to honor that env override, and documented the override path
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not add log rotation, does not mutate `logging.file` in config, and does not change file-log retention or max-size behavior

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61295
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the logger already honored config-backed `logging.file`, but the root CLI startup path only promoted `--log-level`, not `--log-file`, and the shared log-path helpers had no `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE` override layer
- Missing detection / guardrail: there were no tests covering root log-file option propagation, env log-file override resolution, or daemon status reporting when the file sink was overridden out-of-band
- Contributing context (if known): several CLI/status/docs surfaces already treated log-file path as configurable, but they were only looking at config/default paths instead of the full effective override stack

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/logging/logger-env.test.ts`, `src/cli/program/preaction.test.ts`, `src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: root `--log-file` should become `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE`, logger resolution should prefer that env path, and daemon status should report the same effective file sink
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the three boundaries that need to agree for users to trust the override: CLI parsing, runtime logger resolution, and status/reporting
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing root-option tests covered `--log-level`, but not file-path overrides
- If no new test is added, why not:

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw --log-file /path/to/openclaw.log ...` now forces the file log sink for that process.
- `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE=/path/to/openclaw.log` now overrides `logging.file`.
- Daemon status/reporting now shows the effective env-overridden log file path instead of only the config/default path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[root CLI invocation]
  -> [--log-level handled]
  -> [--log-file ignored]
  -> [logger/status fall back to config/default path]

After:
[root CLI invocation with --log-file]
  -> [preaction exports OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE]
  -> [logger + status helpers prefer env log path]
  -> [runtime and reported file sink stay aligned]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm/vitest
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): root CLI + logger bootstrap + daemon status
- Relevant config (redacted): default config with per-process log file override via root CLI flag or `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE`

### Steps

1. Run `openclaw --log-file /tmp/custom-openclaw.log status` or export `OPENCLAW_LOG_FILE=/tmp/custom-openclaw.log`.
2. Start or inspect a gateway/CLI command that writes file logs.
3. Check the resolved log file path from runtime/status surfaces.

### Expected

- The process should write logs to `/tmp/custom-openclaw.log`, and status helpers should report that same path.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no root `--log-file` path, and env-based log-file overrides were not part of the resolved logger/status path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

target.title: fix(msteams): paginate thread replies and keep recent context
target.body:
Title: fix(msteams): paginate thread replies and keep recent context

## Summary

- Problem: Teams thread context enrichment only used the first Graph replies page, so long threads could skew toward the oldest replies.
- Why it matters: the bot could miss the newest thread context, which is usually the most relevant context for a channel-thread reply.
- What changed: `fetchThreadReplies` now follows `@odata.nextLink` under a hard page cap and returns the most recent bounded reply window instead of only the first page.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no live-tenant Graph validation, no changes to higher-level message-handler behavior, and no prompt-formatting changes beyond the selected reply window.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the Teams thread helper fetched only the initial Graph replies page even when Graph exposed additional pages via `@odata.nextLink`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit coverage for paginated reply retrieval or for preferring the newest bounded reply window.
- Contributing context (if known): the Graph replies endpoint is oldest-first and does not support `$orderby`, so a single-page implementation silently favors stale context on long threads.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when Graph returns multiple reply pages for a channel thread, the helper follows pagination and keeps the most recent bounded reply window rather than only the first page.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to the Graph reply helper and can be reproduced deterministically with mocked paginated Graph responses.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Long Teams channel threads can now contribute newer reply context to the bot instead of only the oldest reply page.
- Behavior stays best-effort: if Graph pagination or fetches fail, the surrounding handler still degrades gracefully as before.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[long Teams thread] -> [fetch first replies page only] -> [oldest replies injected] -> [bot may miss latest context]

After:
[long Teams thread] -> [follow paginated replies with hard cap] -> [keep most recent bounded window] -> [bot sees newer thread context]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (Yes)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Risk: this can issue additional Graph requests for long threads.
  - Mitigation: pagination is capped at 10 pages and the helper still returns only the requested bounded reply window.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: repo-local Node via `pnpm openclaw`
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): MSTeams Graph helper unit tests
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Mock the first Graph replies page with `reply-1`, `reply-2`, and an `@odata.nextLink`.
2. Mock the second page with `reply-3`, `reply-4`.
3. Call `fetchThreadReplies(..., limit=2)`.

### Expected

- The helper should follow the next link and return the newest bounded window: `reply-3`, `reply-4`.

### Actual

- Before the fix, the helper would only use the first page and return `reply-1`, `reply-2`, dropping the newer page entirely.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: targeted unit run for `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.test.ts`; paginated reply path; hard page-cap path; existing thread-helper tests remained green.
- Edge cases checked: missing `value`, min/max limit clamping, bounded pagination cap.
- What you did **not** verify: live Microsoft Graph behavior against a real Teams tenant or real long thread pagination ordering/latency.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Microsoft Graph pagination behavior for long Teams threads has not been validated against a real tenant in this change.
  - Mitigation: the PR makes the assumption explicit, keeps the change local to the helper, and adds deterministic regression tests around the intended pagination behavior.
- Risk: extra Graph fetches could increase latency on large threads.
  - Mitigation: page following is hard-capped and still returns only a bounded recent window.

- If no new test is added, why not:

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

target.title: Fix commentary leaks in OpenResponses HTTP streaming
target.body:
## Summary
- stop streaming assistant commentary deltas to OpenResponses HTTP clients before the phase is known
- keep completed OpenResponses output items tagged with the right phase so commentary + tool-call turns still preserve structure
- add regression coverage for commentary suppression and explicit final-answer streaming

## What changed
The OpenResponses HTTP SSE path was forwarding every `assistant` delta immediately, even when the turn later finalized as `phase: "commentary"` for a tool call. That leaked internal planning text like tool-call scaffolding to end users.

This patch buffers assistant text until the phase is known:
- `commentary` deltas stay buffered and are not emitted as `response.output_text.delta`
- `final_answer` deltas are emitted normally
- completed output items still carry the expected `phase` metadata so tool-call turns remain structured correctly

## Tests
Passed:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts src/gateway/openresponses-phase.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

Known unrelated repo baseline failures observed while validating:
- `pnpm check` fails on existing type errors outside this patch (for example in `extensions/discord`, `extensions/feishu`, `extensions/nextcloud-talk`, `extensions/whatsapp`, `src/cron`, `src/mcp`, `src/wizard`)
- `pnpm test` also hits unrelated existing failures / resource issues outside this patch (for example `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts`, `src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts`, and an out-of-memory worker termination)

target.title: agents/context: unlock context1m on proxied Anthropic 1M-capable models (#69353)
target.body:
Fixes #69353.

### Summary
`isAnthropic1MModel` in `src/agents/context.ts` gates the 1M context window on `provider === "anthropic"`, so `context1m: true` was a silent no-op for any provider proxying Claude Opus 4 / Sonnet 4 — notably the bundled `github-copilot` provider, which routes to the same underlying Anthropic models but identifies itself as `"github-copilot"`.

This change keeps the prefix check (`claude-opus-4*`, `claude-sonnet-4*`) but drops the provider guard, so `context1m: true` works across proxying providers. The model-id prefix is still the authoritative filter, so non-Anthropic-capable models (e.g. `claude-haiku-3-5`) stay at their configured / fallback window.

### Why
Users configuring `agents.defaults.models["github-copilot/claude-opus-4-6"].params.context1m = true` observed sessions still compacting at ~180k tokens. The user opt-in is an explicit trust signal; the provider guard was over-restrictive.

### Implementation
- `src/agents/context.ts`: rename helper to `isAnthropic1MCapableModel(model)` and drop the unused `provider` parameter. The prefix filter (`ANTHROPIC_1M_MODEL_PREFIXES = ["claude-opus-4", "claude-sonnet-4"]`) remains the source of truth. Precedent: `extensions/anthropic/stream-wrappers.ts` already defines an identically-scoped `isAnthropic1MModel(modelId)` that only looks at the model id.
- `src/agents/context.test.ts`: two regression tests.
  1. `github-copilot` + `claude-opus-4-6` + `context1m: true` → returns 1M.
  2. `github-copilot` + `claude-haiku-3-5` + `context1m: true` → still returns 200k (prefix gate intact).

### Verification
- `pnpm test src/agents/context.test.ts` → 15/15 (13 pre-existing + 2 new).
- No unintentional behavior change for `provider === "anthropic"` callers; all prior assertions in `context.test.ts` still pass unchanged.
- Pre-existing `src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts` type errors on `origin/main` are unrelated (upstream commit 16985aba4e, not in this diff).

### Notes
- Backwards compatible. `context1m` remains an explicit opt-in, the prefix filter is unchanged, and the fallback paths (per-model `contextTokens`, cached/discovery windows) are untouched.
- AI-assisted contribution.

target.title: fix(mcp): allow SecretRef values in mcp.servers env and headers [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds SecretRef support to core MCP server configuration so `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` can use SecretInput values in addition to plaintext literals.

Before this change, core `mcp.servers` only accepted primitive literal values on these surfaces, which meant MCP credentials on the built-in config path had to remain plaintext even though other SecretRef-capable surfaces already supported resolved runtime credentials.

## What changed

- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.env.*`
- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.headers.*`
- include core MCP env/header targets in command/runtime secret resolution
- resolve only the active MCP transport surface at runtime
- include core MCP env/header targets in the supported SecretRef target registry
- teach `secrets configure` and `secrets audit` to handle MCP env/header targets without promoting generic non-secret literals like URLs and feature flags
- update the MCP and SecretRef docs/matrix to document the supported surface

## Why

This keeps sensitive MCP credentials out of plaintext config while preserving the existing runtime model:

- config stores SecretRefs
- activation resolves active refs into the in-memory runtime snapshot
- spawned MCP processes receive resolved string values at launch time

That is consistent with the rest of OpenClaw’s SecretRef model and removes a real gap on the core `mcp.servers` surface.

## Validation

Passed locally:

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`

Passed targeted coverage for the touched surface:

- `src/config/config-misc.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/configure-plan.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-core-snapshots.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-inactive-core-surfaces.test.ts`
- `src/commands/agent.runtime-config.test.ts`
- `src/cli/command-secret-targets.test.ts`
- `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- targeted `src/secrets/audit.test.ts` coverage for MCP plaintext detection heuristics

Additional functional validation:

- verified a core stdio MCP server configured through `mcp.servers.*.env.*` receives resolved string env values at launch time while the config itself still stores SecretRef objects
- verified custom credential-like names such as `SERVICE_KEY` and `X-Access-Key` are treated as sensitive by configure/audit flows
- verified generic literals such as URLs and feature-flag headers are not promoted as secret targets

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` only accepted primitive string literals before this PR. Passing a SecretRef object (`{"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}`) as an MCP env or header value caused `config validate` to return `Invalid input`. This PR widens the accepted surface so both `env` and `headers` fields on core MCP servers store SecretInput values, with runtime resolution delivering the resolved string to the spawned MCP process.

- Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04 (HAL server), OpenClaw built from PR head `24e3416d75b` (runtime captured at parent `9598ab1` — `24e3416d75b` changes test-file module paths only, no runtime delta). 1Password CLI service account for secret backing.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Write `openclaw.json` with `mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY = {"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}` and `mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization = {"source":"env","id":"MC_AUTH_TOKEN"}`
  2. `openclaw config validate`
  3. `openclaw secrets audit` (env vars absent — SecretRefs unresolved)
  4. `MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit`

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output, values redacted):

  ```
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (9598ab1)

  $ openclaw config validate
  Config valid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json

  $ openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: unresolved. plaintext=0, unresolved=3, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_API_KEY (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_AUTH_TOKEN (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:models.providers.anthropic.apiKey
      Failed to resolve env:default:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (variable missing)

  $ MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: clean. plaintext=0, unresolved=0, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  ```

- Observed result after fix: Config validates cleanly with SecretRef objects in both `env.*` and `headers.*`. Secrets audit identifies `MC_API_KEY` and `Authorization` as unresolved SecretRefs and correctly ignores `MC_URL` (URL-shaped) and `X-Feature-Flag` (generic literal). With env vars set, audit is fully clean (`plaintext=0, unresolved=0`).

- What was not tested: Live end-to-end MCP spawn with SecretRef values resolved from exec provider (HAL `/tmp` is mounted `noexec`, blocking shell-script exec tests locally). MCP session runtime SecretRef injection is covered by unit tests in `pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `/tmp` is mounted `noexec` on the test machine, preventing exec-provider shell-script invocation locally. Same exec tests pass in CI (GitHub Actions standard tmpfs is executable). Version shown is `9598ab1` (parent of `24e3416d75b`) — both commits produce identical runtime behavior; `24e3416d75b` only renames test-file module paths.

- Before evidence: Without this PR, `openclaw config validate` on the same config returned:
  ```
  OpenClaw config is invalid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY: Invalid input
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization: Invalid input
  ```

## Scope

This PR is limited to core MCP SecretRef support for `mcp.servers` env/header surfaces and the related docs/tests needed to support that behavior.

It does not include unrelated runtime, deployment, or integration changes.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted.

Testing performed locally with repo checks, targeted tests, and direct runtime validation.

target.title: fix(tui): preserve spaces between thinking fragments
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: streamed thinking fragments were joined with single newlines, and the TUI markdown renderer collapsed those boundaries so adjacent sentences appeared smushed together
- Why it matters: reasoning output in the terminal became hard to read even though the underlying content arrived correctly
- What changed: normalize single-line thinking boundaries into spaces while preserving paragraph breaks, and add formatter/stream-assembler regressions
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): assistant answer text rendering and non-thinking text block joining remain unchanged

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #66008
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the TUI formatter preserved single newlines between thinking fragments, but the markdown renderer did not render those boundaries as readable spaces
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering multiple thinking blocks rendered in a single streamed assistant update
- Contributing context (if known): reasoning/thinking often arrives in sentence-sized chunks instead of one complete paragraph

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: src/tui/tui-formatters.test.ts and src/tui/tui-stream-assembler.test.ts
- Scenario the test should lock in: consecutive thinking fragments stay readable with spaces while explicit paragraph breaks survive
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact formatter and streamed-assembly surfaces that build terminal assistant output
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): prior tests covered thinking visibility but not fragment boundary spacing
- If no new test is added, why not:

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Streamed thinking text in the TUI now keeps sentence spacing instead of smushing adjacent fragments together.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[thinking chunk A]\n[thinking chunk B] -> markdown render -> "chunk Achunk B"

After:
[thinking chunk A]\n[thinking chunk B] -> whitespace normalization -> "chunk A chunk B"
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm/vitest
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): TUI
- Relevant config (redacted): showThinking=true

### Steps

1. Render an assistant message containing multiple thinking fragments.
2. Show thinking in the TUI.
3. Inspect the rendered output.

### Expected

- Adjacent thinking fragments remain readable with spaces between sentences.

### Actual

- Before this fix, sentence boundaries could collapse and appear as one long smushed block.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: targeted vitest run for src/tui/tui-formatters.test.ts and src/tui/tui-stream-assembler.test.ts
- Edge cases checked: consecutive thinking fragments and explicit paragraph breaks
- What you did **not** verify: full interactive terminal reproduction against a live model stream

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: some intentionally line-broken thinking text could become single-paragraph prose
  - Mitigation: explicit blank-line paragraph breaks are preserved, and the fix only touches thinking text in the TUI path

AI-assisted: Yes (Codex). Testing: targeted vitest coverage listed above. I attempted codex review --base origin/main, but the local Codex CLI is currently rate-limited.

target.title: fix(telegram): keep ack reactions without mentions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram ack reactions stop when group `requireMention` is disabled, even though those group messages still trigger runs.
- Why it matters: always-on Telegram groups look ignored while the agent is actually working.
- What changed: when Telegram is using the default `messages.ackReactionScope: "group-mentions"`, groups with `requireMention: false` now keep ack reactions; added a focused regression test and docs note.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no change to non-Telegram channels, and no change to explicit ack scopes such as `all`, `direct`, `group-all`, `off`, or `none`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #65808
- Related #65808
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the shared ack-reaction gate required `requireMention=true` for `group-mentions`, so Telegram groups configured to process unmentioned messages stopped qualifying for the ack emoji.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering ack reactions in Telegram groups with `requireMention: false`.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram already treats those messages as reply-worthy once mention gating is disabled, so the ack surface drifted from actual processing behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a group message without a mention still receives the ack reaction when Telegram group config sets `requireMention: false`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior depends on the Telegram message pipeline, config resolution, and reaction dispatch, so the existing create-bot harness is the smallest seam that exercises the full path.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the adjacent mention-gated ack reaction test in the same file.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram groups configured with `requireMention: false` now keep the ack emoji while the message is being processed, even when the global ack scope remains `group-mentions`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[group message, no mention] -> [Telegram run starts] -> [no ack reaction shown]

After:
[group message, no mention] -> [Telegram run starts] -> [ack reaction shown] -> [reply arrives]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.ackReaction="👀"`, `messages.ackReactionScope="group-mentions"`, `channels.telegram.groups["*"].requireMention=false`

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram with `messages.ackReaction` enabled and `channels.telegram.groups["*"].requireMention=false`.
2. Send a normal group message without mentioning the bot.
3. Observe whether the ack reaction appears before the reply is delivered.

### Expected

- The bot acknowledges the inbound group message because that message is eligible to trigger a run.

### Actual

- Before this change, no ack reaction was sent for that message path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused verification after the patch:

```text
$ npx --yes pnpm exec vitest run extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       51 passed (51)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: mention-gated Telegram group messages still ack; Telegram groups with `requireMention: false` now ack without a mention.
- Edge cases checked: the change is gated to Telegram and only rewrites the effective scope when the current scope is `group-mentions`.
- What you did **not** verify: live Telegram Bot API behavior against a real bot account; multi-account Telegram setups.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Telegram users who intentionally paired `requireMention: false` with `group-mentions` may now see more ack reactions in always-on groups.
  - Mitigation: the change is limited to Telegram groups already configured to process every visible message, and users can still opt into `off`, `none`, `direct`, `group-all`, or `all` explicitly.
